name: Sweetgreen
description: Fast-casual food reference — warm earthen cream and brick-clay backgrounds anchored by deep ink text, vegetal-green signature accent for CTAs. Humanist sans for trust, serif moments for menu storytelling. The palette reads as 'grown, not manufactured'.

--color-cream-base.role: Primary light surface — page background, menu card fills

--color-ink-anchor.role: Primary text on cream + dark sections — reads as black but rooted in green

--color-clover-spark.role: Signature accent — CTA buttons, key links, leaf iconography

--color-brick-accent.role: Secondary accent — limited-time menu callouts, seasonal flags
--color-brick-accent.context: warm earthen clay, used as a punctuation

--color-sage-muted.role: Tertiary accents, decorative botanical elements, low-emphasis tags

--color-shadow-surface.role: Alternating section background — menu grid, secondary surfaces

--font-display-serif.role: Display headlines — menu names, hero, editorial titling for ingredient stories

--font-body-humanist.role: Body copy, navigation, menu item descriptions, nutrition labels

antiPatterns: Never pair --color-clover-spark and --color-brick-accent in the same section — they fight for the accent role. Pick one per section; brick is for limited-time-only, clover is the default.
Never set --color-cream-base against pure white — the cream is the brand. Adjacent pure-white surfaces make the cream read as 'dirty paper' instead of 'warm hospitality'.
Display headlines must use --font-display-serif for menu storytelling moments — humanist sans at display sizes reads as fast food, not fast-casual. Serif is the up-market signal.
Avoid pure black (#000) anywhere — Sweetgreen's deepest tone is forest-ink (#0F1F0A). True black breaks the 'grown not manufactured' palette story.
